Styles › Biophilic

2010s – present · Global

Biophilic

Architecture woven with living nature.

  • Overview
  • Design Philosophy
  • Visual Characteristics
  • Materials
  • Color Palette
  • Façade Guide
  • Lighting
  • Building Types
  • Design Checklist
  • Common Mistakes
  • AI Prompts
  • Gallery
  • Related

01 — Overview

Overview

Definition. Biophilic design puts living nature at the centre of the exterior — green walls, planted terraces, water and natural materials that reconnect people with the living world and improve wellbeing.

Historical background. Building on sustainability and Edward Wilson's 'biophilia', it turned greenery, daylight and natural texture into primary design elements for healthier cities.

Origin. Global; advanced by tropical and European practices.

Timeline

2014

Bosco Verticale's vertical forest

2013

Parkroyal Pickering's sky-gardens

2018

WELL/biophilic standards spread

2021

Green high-rise mainstream

Key Architects

Stefano BoeriWOHAJean NouvelPatrick Blanc

Influential Projects

Bosco Verticale

Stefano Boeri · 2014
Milan, Italy

Parkroyal Collection Pickering

WOHA · 2013
Singapore

One Central Park

Jean Nouvel · 2014
Sydney, Australia

02 — Design Philosophy

Design Philosophy

Core Principles

Living nature firstGreenery & waterNatural materials & lightWellbeingHabitat & biodiversity

The Thinking

The façade becomes a living system — planted, watered and textured — dissolving the boundary between building and ecosystem to restore people's connection with nature.

Values It Represents

WellbeingRestorationBiodiversityHarmony

Why It Emerged

Rising urban density and evidence that contact with nature improves health drove nature to the centre of architecture.

03 — Visual Characteristics

Visual Characteristics

MassingTerraced, layered volumes shaped to hold planting and bring in light.
Form LanguageClean forms softened and overgrown by living greenery.
CompositionPlanted balconies, terraces and green walls articulate the façade.
ProportionHuman-scaled with deep planted zones.
GeometryOrthogonal or gently organic, optimised for planting and light.
RhythmRepetitive planters, balconies and trees.
SymmetryAsymmetrical, driven by sun and growth.
ScaleHouse to high-rise; greenery at every scale.
Roof TypesGreen roofs, sky-gardens and planted terraces.
Window ProportionGenerous glazing paired with deep planted balconies.
EntranceOpen, planted, daylit thresholds.
Façade ArticulationLiving green walls, planted terraces, timber and stone with water.

04 — Materials

Materials

Greenery / planting

Living planting that cools, shades and reconnects nature.

Timber

Warm, renewable and tactile — softens and clads the exterior.

Natural stone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Water

Reflective water that cools and animates the exterior.

Glass

Admits light and dissolves the wall between inside and out.

05 — Color Palette

Color Palette

Lush, fresh and restorative — greens and earth tones alive with planting.

Primary

Foliage Green
#5a7048
Warm Timber
#b8915c

Accent

Moss
#46584b
Water Blue
#4f7e86

Material Colors

Stone
#bca588
Glass
#a6c0c8

Typical Combinations

  • +Green + timber + water
  • +Foliage + stone + glass

06 — Façade Design Guide

Façade Design Guide

Window CompositionGenerous glazing paired with deep planted balconies framing greenery and view.
EmphasisLayered depth — planters, trees and green walls.
Shading DevicesPlanting, deep balconies, pergolas and screens provide living shade.
BalconiesDeep planted terraces as habitat and shaded outdoor rooms.
FramesTimber or recycled-content frames.
OpeningsLarge, daylit, screened by greenery.
Architectural DetailsGreen walls, irrigation, planters and trellises.
OrnamentationLiving greenery is the ornament.

07 — Lighting

Lighting

Natural Lighting Strategy

Daylight filtered and softened by planting; greenery shades and cools.

Artificial Lighting Concept

Low-energy, warm, layered; lit greenery and water at night.

Night Façade Appearance

Soft glow with illuminated living façades and gardens.

Recommendations

Efficient warm LED; light planting subtly; avoid skyglow; emphasise green layers.

08 — Typical Building Types

Typical Building Types

Where this style performs best:

Residential towersOfficesHospitals & wellnessSchoolsHotelsMixed-use

09 — Design Checklist

Design Checklist

Tap an item to mark it complete — saved to your browser.

  • Make living nature a primary element
  • Plant balconies, terraces and green walls
  • Integrate water and natural materials
  • Pair glazing with deep planted zones
  • Plan irrigation and maintenance
  • Maximise daylight and views to greenery
  • Design for habitat and biodiversity
  • Keep a green, natural palette

10 — Common Mistakes

Common Mistakes

Elements that contradict the style — what to avoid:

  • ✕Token planting on a sealed glass box (greenwashing)
  • ✕No irrigation or maintenance strategy
  • ✕Greenery that blocks all daylight
  • ✕Ignoring habitat and species choice
  • ✕Synthetic materials denying the natural intent

11 — AI Prompt Examples

AI Prompt Examples

Optimised for Midjourney, ChatGPT image tools or Claude — exterior renders. Tap copy to use.

Exterior

Biophilic residential tower exterior, lush planted balconies and green walls, mature trees on terraces, timber and stone, water features, daylight, architectural photography --ar 2:3 --style raw

Façade Concept

Biophilic façade, deep planted balconies, green wall, timber screens, trees, abundant greenery, frontal elevation, natural light

Night View

Biophilic building exterior at night, softly lit living façade and gardens, warm glow, greenery, architectural night photography

Aerial View

Aerial of a biophilic building, green roofs and tree-filled terraces, planted courtyards, surrounding parkland, drone photography

Street Perspective

Street view of a biophilic building exterior, cascading green balconies, planted entrance, trees, people, documentary photography

Masterplan

Biophilic district masterplan, green roofs, planted streets, water network, tree canopy, blue-green corridors, axonometric, green palette
